title: Observation of associative-memory retrieval and spin-glass phases on a photonic quantum simulator
authors: Taira Giordani, Gennaro Zanfardino, Luca Leuzzi, Enrico Bonfissuto, Eugenio Caruccio, Gabriele Gasbarri, Mattia Bossi, Abhiram Rajan, Riccardo Albiero, Francesco Ceccarelli, Nicolò Spagnolo, Raffaele Santagati, Stefano Paesani, Marco Leonetti, Roberto Osellame, Giorgio Parisi, Giancarlo Ruocco, Fabrizio Illuminati, Fabio Sciarrino
abstract: Models of interacting complex systems provide the fundamental statistical physics reference frame for the study and the understanding of associative memories, machine learning, and the dynamics of neural networks. On the other hand, simulating complex multi-synaptic interactions on a classical hardware is computationally demanding due to the super-linear scaling of the system complexity. Photonic quantum technologies provide a promising solution to these limitations by leveraging on their inherent speed and parallel processing ability in order to simulate complex networks. Recently, a connection between multiphoton processes and generalized $p$-body Hopfield models has been theoretically established. Here, we design and demonstrate an experimental platform that exploits single photons distributed across a set of optical modes, in which controlled arrays of binary phase shifters act as Ising-like neurons. We focus specifically on a fully connected Hopfield Hamiltonian with four-body local interaction terms, realized via two-photon processes. Through quantum simulations on programmable photonic processors, the study identifies three distinct regimes: a memory retrieval phase, a spin-glass memory "black-out" phase, and a paramagnetic phase. Experimental results confirm successful memory retrieval at low storage capacities and temperatures, where the system consistently relaxes to fixed points with high memory overlap, effectively reconstructing the stored patterns. Future research will extend the platform design to investigate networks with local or dilute interactions, while advances in the realization of scalable photonic circuits will enable architectures that encompass very large numbers of interacting spins.
summary: This paper presents an experimental photonic quantum simulator that emulates a generalized Hopfield neural network. By using two-photon interference, the platform successfully observes the transition between memory retrieval, spin-glass, and paramagnetic phases. This demonstrates the potential of photonic technologies to solve complex optimization and many-body simulation problems.
main_problem: Simulating the complex, multi-synaptic interactions of large-scale neural networks and spin glasses is computationally expensive on classical hardware due to super-linear scaling.
main_result: The researchers experimentally demonstrated a photonic quantum simulator capable of identifying three distinct phases: memory retrieval, spin-glass 'black-out', and paramagnetic phases.
method: The study uses a programmable photonic processor to implement Metropolis Monte Carlo sampling, where two-photon interference is used to evaluate the energy of spin configurations.
model_or_system: A programmable quantum photonic simulator (QOLOSSUS) using 8- and 12-mode integrated circuits, implementing a $p=4$ body Hopfield Hamiltonian via two-photon processes.
key_observables: Memory overlap (bilinear magnetization $|m|$), spin-spin overlap between replicas ($q$), and spin-spin autocorrelation function.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), storage capacity ($\alpha$), and number of spins/modes ($M$).
main_assumptions: The current implementation is limited to small-scale architectures (6- and 10-spin systems).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the simulator setup and the Metropolis optimization loop; Figure 2 details the experimental apparatus and photon-pair generation; Figure 5 presents the reconstructed $(\alpha, T)$ phase diagram; Figure 6 shows magnetization and overlap distributions for the emulated Ising-like model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of classical neural network simulation, describes the photonic hardware and the mathematical mapping of the Hopfield model to photonics, details the experimental validation of the setup, presents the characterization of the phase diagram, and concludes with future scaling directions.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a powerful application of quantum optics and photonics for simulating complex many-body dynamics and phase transitions in disordered systems, bridging the gap between quantum hardware and statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. Quantum photonic spin-simulator. a) Overview of QOLOSSUS’s overall apparatus. This setup includes a single photon source based on quantum dot technology and a time-to-space demultiplexing system (DMX) to synchronize two-photon states. The apparatus simulates spin dynamics within fully programmable integrated circuits. It also includes a detection and data processing stage to estimate the system energy and propose a new spin update according to the Metropolis algorithm. b) Details of the apparatus for the generation of the two-photon states. This stage comprises a pulsed laser to excite the quantum dot single photon source. The DMX system is followed by time delays to synchronize...

Experimental characterization of the 10-spin simulator within the 12-mode integrated device. a) Histograms of the 1024 TVD values between the theoretical and the two-photon experimental distributions for each spin configuration and for a fixed 10 × 10 scattering matrix S. b-c) Details on the experimental two-photon distributions for two spin settings. TVD is 0.139 ± 0.002 in b) and 0.057 ± 0.002 in c). We selected NP = 7 (α = 0.07) and...

Figure 5. Phase Diagram of the 10-spin simulator. The α vs T phase diagram was reconstructed from 360 simulated points obtained over 100 different disorder realizations, corresponding to distinct measured scattering matrices S. Specifically, for each point (α, T) and for each realization of S, Nrep = 100 independent replicas were generated to reconstruct the overlap distributions P(q) and the magnetization distribution P(|m|), from which the memory retrieval (MR, red), spin-glass (SG, blue), and paramagnetic (PM, green) phases were identified. In the P(|m|) plots the vertical line is |m|NP = 1/π (see text). The phase boundaries were determined by identifying the transition between phases...

Figure 6. Emulation of memory retrieval effects of the classical Hopfield model. Experimental results for a scat- tering matrix featuring two rows with entries ±1 up to an overall normalization factors. The photonic spin-simulator displays the typical magnetization and order parameter q be- havior expected for a 4-body Hopfield model with respectively 1 stored pattern (above) and 2 orthogonal stored patterns (be- low). The table on the left showcases the joint probability of Im(m) and Re(m): in both cases m is real and only takes val- ues m = ±1 and m = 0.
